domanda: 1. In the past, Wide Area Networks (WAN):
opzioneA: Were intended as geographic networks designed mainly for low bitrate applications
opzioneB: Were intended as shared networks designed mainly to exchange large amount of data
opzioneC: Were intended as geographic networks designed mainly to exchange large amount of data
opzioneD: Were intended as campus networks designed mainly to connect several buildings to each other

domanda: 2. In the past, Local Area Networks (LAN):
opzioneA: Were intended as local networks designed mainly for low bitrate applications
opzioneB: Were intended as shared networks designed mainly to exchange large amount of data
opzioneC: Were intended as shared networks designed mainly for "bursty" application
opzioneD: Were intended as campus networks designed mainly to connect several buildings to each other

domanda: 3. In networks based on a shared communication medium:
opzioneA: While a station is transmitting, other stations cannot transmit
opzioneB: While a station is transmitting, other stations can transmit only if the previous communica- tion is not directed to them
opzioneC: While a station is transmitting, other stations can receive and transmit at the same time
opzioneD: All stations can transmit at any time without any risk of collision thanks to the presence of the switch

domanda: 4. In a LAN:
opzioneA: There is always a intermediate device that handles the frames
opzioneB: All devices have the same priority in getting access to the shared communication medium
opzioneC: All devices have the same priority in getting access to the shared communication medium, except a possible intermediate device that is privileged against other stations
opzioneD: If needed, we can configure a possible device (e.g. server) that has higher priority in getting access to the shared communication medium

domanda: 5. A shared communication medium technology is characterized by:
opzioneA: Broadcast communication, no intermediate sistems and high flexibility
opzioneB: Low cost, thanks to the deployment of level 2 communication devices (L2 Switch)
opzioneC: Reliability, privacy reasonably guaranteed for users, but impossibility to have multiple communications at the same time between hosts
opzioneD: Reliability, possibility to have multiple communications at the same time between hosts, but degree of privacy

domanda: 6. The OSI level named "Data Link":
opzioneA: Assumes that physical-layer functions are defined, which are the ones used to allow several networks technologies (e.g. Ethernet, WiFI) to communicate together
opzioneB: Can implements multicast/broadcast addresses that can be exploited for Solicitation and Advertisement functions
opzioneC: Defines how to generate bits over the channel
opzioneD: Can be divided into a LCC (Link Central Control) sublevel and a MAC (Medium Access Control) sublevel

domanda: 7. The OSI level named "Physical":
opzioneA: It is specified only for Ethernet networks
opzioneB: It is the same in all cabled networks (e.g. Ethernet, Token Ring, FDDI), and basically is derived from the specifications of the original Ethernet
opzioneC: It can be shared among several network technologies, witch however differ on Data-Link level (e.g., Ethernet and FDDI)
opzioneD: It defines the way to get access to the shared communication medium

domanda: 8. The LLC (Logical Link Control) sublevel:
opzioneA: It has almost disappeared from modern networks
opzioneB: It was never able to succeed on WiFi networks, which use another framing format instead
opzioneC: It is present in a few protocols operating on Ethernet networks
opzioneD: It defines 64 bit addresses to identify the level 3 protocol contained in the frame

domanda: 9. The MAC (Medium Access Control) sublevel:
opzioneA: It defines the arbitration mechanism in order to get access to the shared communication medium
opzioneB: It defines a field named "Protocol Type" that identifies the level 3 protocol contained in the frame
opzioneC: It is present in all LAN technologies, except in Ethernet where is usually replaced by LLC-SNAP
opzioneD: It defines how bits must transmitted over the physical channel

domanda: 10. MAC addresses:
opzioneA: Are allocated by government authorities
opzioneB: Are set by the user (or operating system) when the devices is used for the first time
opzioneC: Are set by the manufacturer: the first 3 bytes identify the vendor and remaining are defined by an algorithm that generates random 24 bits numbers
opzioneD: Are set by the manufacturer in each network interface card

domanda: 11. If the operating system of a station receives all the packets that are in fact received by the network interface card:
opzioneA: The station does not have an IP address
opzioneB: The station is connected to a switch
opzioneC: The station has a multicast MAC address
opzioneD: The network card is in "promiscuous" mode

domanda: 12. The difference between "frame" and "packet" is:
opzioneA: A frame identifies level 2 data, whereas a packet identify level 3 data
opzioneB: A frame identifies level 3 data, whereas a packet identify level 2 data
opzioneC: They are synonymous
opzioneD: A frame identifies Ethernet data, whereas a packet identify other protocols data

domanda: 13. If a host is connected for the first time to a shared Ethernet LAN and the user types the PING command toward an other station:
opzioneA: It generates immediately and ICMP Echo Request packet toward the destination, since we are on a shared communication medium
opzioneB: It generates first an ARP packet in order to know the destination MAC address of the destination host, that will reply with its IP address
opzioneC: If the destination host belongs to a different level 3 network, it must generate a packet that discovers the MAC address of its default gateway
opzioneD: It generates an ARP Request, that has the side effect of updating the filtering database of all stations

domanda: 14. A host "A" is turned on and connected for the first time to a shared Ethernet LAN. Then, the user types the PING command toward the server "S" "www.polito.it", which is connected to the same LAN. The first frame that will be generated on the network will be:
opzioneA: An ARP Request toward the DNS if "S" belongs to a different level 3 network
opzioneB: A DNS Query toward the DNS if "A" belongs to the same IP network of the DNS and the router
opzioneC: An ARP Request containing the router IP address in the "Target IP Address" field if "S" belongs to a different IP network compared to the host
opzioneD: An ARP Request containing the router IP address in the "Target IP Address" field if the DNS server belongs to a different IP network compared to the host

domanda: 15. A frame that contains the MAC address of a router as destination address:
opzioneA: It will have the IP address of the router as destination IP address
opzioneB: It will have the IP address of a station does not belong to local LAN as destination IP address
opzioneC: It will have the IP address of a station that belongs to local LAN as destination IP address
opzioneD: It will have the IP address of the router or the one of a station that does not belong to the same IP network of the source host as destination IP address

domanda: 16. Before an ICMP Echo Request packet, the trasmitting station:
opzioneA: It always generates an ARP Request
opzioneB: It always generates a DNS Query
opzioneC: It always generates an ARP Request and a DNS Query
opzioneD: It does not necessarily generate additional packets

domanda: 17. Main characteristics of a physical copper link, with respect to data transmission are:
opzioneA: Propagation speed (expressed as a fraction of the light speed), impedance of the line (expressed in AWG, American Wire Gage), size of the physical copper wire
opzioneB: Impedance of the line (expressed in AWG, American Wire Gage), size of the physical copper wire
opzioneC: Propagation speed (expressed as a fraction of the light speed), impedance of the line, size of the physical copper wire (expressed in AWG, American Wire Gage)
opzioneD: Propagation speed (expressed as a fraction of the light speed), impedance of the line, link length (expressed in AWG, American Wire Gage)

domanda: 18. The AWG (American Wire Gage):
opzioneA: Measures the signal attenuation
opzioneB: Indicates the maximum noise tolerated by the cable
opzioneC: Measures the size of the physical copper wire
opzioneD: Measures the size of the core of a fiber optic link

domanda: 19. The Cross-Talk:
opzioneA: Is called "Alien" if it generated by a generic external source of noise
opzioneB: Is due to attenuation of a signal into a copper wire
opzioneC: Is due to interference generated by the other physical links in the cable
opzioneD: It can be present if a plug is installed in the wrong way or if the wire has several curves on its path

domanda: 20. With respect to fiber optic cables:
opzioneA: Multimode fibers with led technology are the most recent and have the highest bandwidth
opzioneB: Multimode fibers have low dispersion rate and high bandwidth
opzioneC: Indoor cables do not need an external metallic shield
opzioneD: Singlemode fibers with laser technology are the most recent and have the highest bandwidth

domanda: 21. In structured cabling:
opzioneA: The structure usually follows a tree, in which several portions are aggregated together at different levels
opzioneB: It is a good rule to locate the Floor Distribution and the Building Distributor in the same room, so they can be connected to each other using short 10Gb cables
opzioneC: Usually a ring is preferred to a tree topology because of its robustness
opzioneD: Copper wires can span up to 100m, plus the additional patch cords at their ends (e.g. user station and network device)

domanda: 22. Copper wires in a data-center:
opzioneA: Are not used due to very high high communication speed requested
opzioneB: Are heavily used throughout the entire data-center mainly for budget reasons
opzioneC: Are used in some portions of the data-center (where cables are reasonably short), mainly for budget reasons
opzioneD: Are usually used to connect disks, which are characterized by lower speed than servers

domanda: 23. When transmitting on an Ethernet network, a station sends a "jamming sequence" if:
opzioneA: The current trasmission was succesfull
opzioneB: It wants to "reinforce" a collision
opzioneC: It wants to take the ownneship of the channel
opzioneD: It wants to release the channel

domanda: 24. After a collision on an Ethernet network:
opzioneA: All the stations can immediately compete for the ownership of the physical channel for a new transmission
opzioneB: The stations that generate the collision (and only these ones) can compete for the ownership of the physical channel
opzioneC: Stations that generated the collision must wait a random time, called "back-off", before competing again for the ownership of the physical channel
opzioneD: Only the station that generated collision (i.e., the station that started transmit later) must wait a random time, called "back-off", before competing again for the ownership of the physical channel

domanda: 25. In order to detect collisions on an Ethernet network, we need to consider:
opzioneA: Distance among stations, minimum frame size, signal propagation speed
opzioneB: Distance among stations, link bandwidth, minimum frame size
opzioneC: Distance among stations, link bandwidth, minimum frame size, signal propagation speed
opzioneD: Number of stations on the network, collision detection speed, minimum duration of the transmission

domanda: 26. To distinguish an Ethernet 2.0 (DIX) frame from an IEEE 802.3 frame:
opzioneA: We can check the "Version" field at the beginning of frame
opzioneB: We can check the value of the 2 bytes corresponding to the "Ethertype" field in Ethernet 2.0 (or "Length" field in IEEE 802.3)
opzioneC: We can calculate the size of the data carried in the frame; in case this value is is lower than 64 bytes, the frame is IEEE 802.3, otherwise it is Ethernet 2.0
opzioneD: We can look at the possible presence of an LLC SNAP envelope, that is only present in the IEE 802.3 frame

domanda: 27. Frames Ethernet 2.0 (DIX) and IEEE 802.3:
opzioneA: Are not compatible and have different sizes for the minimum frame
opzioneB: Are compatible, even if they have different sizes for the minimum frame
opzioneC: Are compatible, even if they have different sizes for the maximum frame
opzioneD: Are compatible, even if only the second can contain the "Padding" field

domanda: 28. In Ethernet, the Inter-frame gap is:
opzioneA: The required silence between a frame and the following
opzioneB: Empty data within the frame, which allow reaching the minimum frame size needed to detect a collision
opzioneC: The set of bytes sent on the channel acting as end frame delimiter
opzioneD: The time needed to send the bytes of the preamble (up to 7 bytes maximum)

domanda: 29. The maximum collision diameter in an Ethernet network:
opzioneA: Can be seen as the maximum length of a link between a host and a hub
opzioneB: Can be seen as the maximum length of a link between a host and a bridge
opzioneC: Does not change even if hubs are replaced by bridges in the network
opzioneD: It is approximately 200m in case of copper cables

domanda: 30. What does it happen to a PC with its network interface in Full Duplex Fixed mode if it is connected to a switch with its network interface in auto-negotiation mode?
opzioneA: The interface of the switch goes in half duplex mode and it may detect some fake collisions
opzioneB: The interface of the switch goes automatically into Full Duplex Mode
opzioneC: PC and switch will never be able to communicate
opzioneD: PC and switch will communicate without problems

opzioneA: At layer 1
opzioneB: At layer 2
opzioneC: At layers 1 & 2
opzioneD: Partially at layer 1 and partially at layer 2

domanda: 32. In an Ethernet network based entirely on switches with all links in Full Duplex Mode, how many collisions we may experience?
opzioneA: It depends on the size of the network
opzioneB: It depends on the number of the switches
opzioneC: It depends on the number of connected PCs

opzioneA: Full duplex
opzioneB: Half duplex

domanda: 35. The network is defined as "switched" when:
opzioneA: It contains only switches and end stations
opzioneB: It contains switches, hubs and end stations
opzioneC: It contains at least one router
opzioneD: It is based on a shared physical medium for communication

domanda: 36. In a switched network:
opzioneA: Loosing frames is a rare event thanks to the high speed of the switch
opzioneB: There are no lost frames thanks to the fact that there are no collisions
opzioneC: The number of lost frames may not be negligible because of congestions on the switches

domanda: 37. Switches:
opzioneA: Usually have a single MAC address for the entire device
opzioneB: Have one MAC address per physical port
opzioneC: Do not have any MAC address (they are "transparent" indeed)

domanda: 38. One of the reasons that motivates a network designer to use VLANs is:
opzioneA: Faster frame forwarding
opzioneB: Broadcast traffic reduction
opzioneC: Simplified management of Spanning Tree

domanda: 39. Two PCs belonging to two different VLANs:
opzioneA: Can communicate normally by sending Ethernet frames directly to the other party
opzioneB: Can never send Ethernet frames directly to the other party
opzioneC: One of the two PCs must use a network interface with VLAN IEEE 802.1q tags support
opzioneD: Both PC must use a network interface with VLAN IEEE 802.1q tags support

domanda: 40. Two PCs directly connected to the same Hub (repeater):
opzioneA: Cannot belong to the different VLAN
opzioneB: Will always belong to the same VLAN
opzioneC: Can belong to different VLANs if they use a network interface compatible with the IEEE 802.1q standard
opzioneD: Can belong to different VLANs if they use a network interface compatible with the IEEE 802.1q standard, but there may be some delivery problem with respect to large packets

opzioneA: The stations cannot exchange data unless a router is being used
opzioneB: The stations cannot exchange data even if a router is being used

opzioneD: The stations can always exchange data even if a router is not being used, but there may be some delivery problem with respect to large packets

domanda: 48. The Spanning Tree protocol is used to:
opzioneA: Transform a network containing meshes into a tree, canceling loops
opzioneB: Manage multiple paths in load-balancing, providing redundancy in the network
opzioneC: Optimize the forwarding process
opzioneD: Update the filtering database

domanda: 49. The Spanning Tree protocol operates sequentially according to the following phases:
opzioneA: Root Ports selection, Root Bridge selection, Designated Ports selection
opzioneB: Root Bridge selection, Designated Ports selection, Root Ports selection
opzioneC: Root Bridge selection, Root Ports selection, Designated Ports selection
opzioneD: Root Ports selection, Designated Ports selection, Root Bridge selection

domanda: 50. The Spanning Tree Protocol can have two types of Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU) frames:
opzioneA: Configuration and Topology Change Notification
opzioneB: Configuration and Topology Advertisement
opzioneC: Topology Change Notification and Topology Advertisement
opzioneD: Root Bridge Election and Topology Advertisement

domanda: 51. During the selection of the Root Bridge, the Spanning Tree process operates by analyzing the value of the following field present in the BPDU:
opzioneA: Root Identifier
opzioneB: Root Path Cost
opzioneC: Port Identifier
opzioneD: Bridge Priority

domanda: 52. According to the standard, the tree obtained by the Spanning Tree Protocol is computed:
opzioneA: Starting from the physical topology of the network, independently from the VLANS
opzioneB: Starting from the already configured VLANS
opzioneC: Starting from the already configured VLANs, creating a different tree for every VLAN
opzioneD: Starting from the physical topology of the network, taking into account the already configured VLANS

domanda: 53. We need the Spanning Tree because:
opzioneA: The learning process in bridges cannot operate when the L2 network contains loops
opzioneB: The forward learning process in bridges is not optimized in case of multiple paths toward the destination
opzioneC: In case the network contains multiple paths, the load balancing algorithm can generate frame reordering problems in the destination host
opzioneD: Frames can circulate forever since the Time-To-Live field in the Ethernet frame is not decremented by bridges

domanda: 54. Given two bridges connected to the same L2 network, in which the Bridge Identifiers are 32768-00:11:22:33:44:55 and 28672-00:22:33:44:55:66:
opzioneA: The first will become the root bridge
opzioneB: The second will become the root bridge
opzioneC: The value 28672 is not a valid Bridge Priority
opzioneD: The second bridge will become root for the VLAN 0, while the first will become root bridge for all the other VLANs

opzioneA: Supports networks that include Layer-1 hubs

opzioneC: Allows to use fiber optic in the network
opzioneD: Allows to use different VLANs in the network

opzioneB: Create a redundant point-to-point connection, without increasing the bandwidth

opzioneD: Active or Inactive

domanda: 82. In the transmission of HSRP messages, the TTL field of the corresponding IP packet is set:
opzioneA: To a random value; when the packet arrives to the router it will be discarded automatically, thus avoiding the forwarding out of the LAN
opzioneB: To value "255" and the routers are forced to discard HSRP messages that contain a TTL different from 255
opzioneC: To value "0", thus avoiding that the packet is forwarded out of the LAN
opzioneD: To value "1", thus avoiding that the packet is forwarded out of the LAN

domanda: 83. In the transmission of VRRP messages, the TTL field of the corresponding IP packet is set:
opzioneA: To a random value; when the packet arrives to the router it will be discarded automatically, thus avoiding the forwarding out of the LAN
opzioneB: To value "255" and the routers are forced to discard HSRP messages that contain a TTL different from 255
opzioneC: To value "0", thus avoiding that the packet is forwarded out of the LAN
opzioneD: To value "1", thus avoiding that the packet is forwarded out of the LAN

domanda: 84. In the HSRP protocol, the load balancing functionality on geographical links:
opzioneA: Is provided automatically
opzioneB: Is provided automatically only for the traffic exiting towards the Internet
opzioneC: Is provided automatically only for the incoming traffic in the LAN

domanda: 85. In case of failure of a geographical link connected to an HSRP router in Active state:
opzioneA: The HSRP protocol might change the status of the interface in Active state when the function "track" is enabled

opzioneC: The HSRP protocol will not change the status of the routers (i.e. the current router will still be Active)
opzioneD: The HSRP protocol will send an ICMP Router Redirect message to all the hosts on the LAN in order to force them to change their default gateway and to use the second router

opzioneA: Can occur when there are asymmetric paths (ingress/ egress) of traffic
opzioneB: Can happen only in case of problems on the Spanning Tree Protocol
opzioneC: Can happen only in case some broadcast frames are transmitted

opzioneA: Send packets only in full-duplex mode
opzioneB: Send packets only in half-duplex mode

opzioneA: Based on information of Layer 4 and lower layers

opzioneA: That are able to compute quickly the routing tables
opzioneB: That are able to manage very big routing table

domanda: 90. In case of a network that uses multilayer switches (L2-L3 switches):
opzioneA: Each link that connects a host to the switch is served by an IP network with netmask/30
opzioneB: Each device (switch) has an IP address for each VLAN

opzioneD: We need to use VLANs

domanda: 91. In case of a network that uses multilayer switches (L2-L3 switches), with the Spanning Tree turned on:
opzioneA: We need first to determine the outcome of Spanning Tree, and then which are the HSRP active/standby routers

domanda: 92. In case of a network that uses multilayer switches (L2-3 switches):
opzioneA: Paths are more optimized if the root bridge overlaps with the possible active router deter- mined by HSRP/VRRP
opzioneB: Paths are more optimized if the root bridge does not overlap with the possible active router determined by HSRP/VRRP
opzioneC: The path determined by the Spanning Tree and the possible active router determined by HSRP/VRRP are indipendent concepts and therefore they do not affect how traffic is propogated over the network
opzioneD: Paths mainly depend on the configuration of level 3 addresses associated with VLANs

domanda: 93. In a Content Delivery Network, the content of a server (e.g. web pages) are replicated and:
opzioneA: Distributed over the network, provided that all servers are physically close together
opzioneB: Distributed over the network, provided that all servers are physically far each other
opzioneC: Distributed over the network, no matter of the physical location of servers
opzioneD: Distributed over the network, provided that all servers belong to the same IP subnet

domanda: 94. In a DNS-based Content Delivery Network:
opzioneA: The DNS answers to users' HTTP requests redirecting them to the nearest cache

opzioneD: The DNS returns an IP address according to the requested DNSname (e.g. "[www.cnn.com](https://www.cnn.com)") and the destination IP address of the client
